title: Dictionaries
subtitle: Look things up by name, just like a real dictionary.
goal: Use dictionaries to store key→value pairs, then add, update, look up, remove, and loop through them safely.

blocks.heading: What is a dictionary? 📒
blocks.html:
A dictionary stores pairs: a key and the value that goes with it. Think of a real dictionary — you look up a word (the key) to find its meaning (the value).

Or a phone book: each name (key) points to a phone number (value). Lists keep things in order by number; dictionaries let you find things by name. 🔑

blocks.heading: Making one with { }
blocks.html:
You build a dictionary with curly braces { }. Each pair looks like "key": value, and pairs are separated by commas.

To look up a value, put the key in square brackets: d["key"].

blocks.caption: A tiny phone book
blocks.explain: phone_book has two pairs. We look up a value by its key: phone_book["Mom"] gives back "555-1234".

blocks.html: Keys go in quotes when they are text: d["Mom"], not d[Mom]. Without quotes Python thinks Mom is a variable!

blocks.heading: Add and change things ✏️
blocks.html: Want to add a new pair or change an old one? Just give a key a value: d["key"] = something. If the key is new, it gets added. If it already exists, the value is updated.

blocks.caption: A game character's stats
blocks.code:
hero = {"name": "Pixel", "hp": 100, "coins": 0}

hero["coins"] = 50      # change a value
hero["level"] = 1       # add a brand new key

print(hero["name"], "has", hero["coins"], "coins")
print("Level:", hero["level"])
blocks.explain: Setting hero["coins"] changed an existing value. Setting hero["level"] added a new pair because that key did not exist yet.

blocks.html: Looking up a key that does not exist crashes with a KeyError. To stay safe, use .get() — it returns None (or a default you pick) instead of crashing.

blocks.caption: Safe lookups, removing, and checking with 'in'
blocks.code:
hero = {"name": "Pixel", "hp": 100}

print(hero.get("hp"))            # 100
print(hero.get("magic", 0))     # missing key, so we get 0

print("hp" in hero)             # True
del hero["hp"]                  # remove the hp pair
print("hp" in hero)             # False now
blocks.explain: .get("magic", 0) returns 0 because there is no magic key — no crash. The word 'in' checks if a key exists. del removes a pair.

blocks.heading: Looping through a dictionary 🔁
blocks.html:
Dictionaries have three handy helpers for loops:

  • .keys() — all the keys
  • .values() — all the values
  • .items() — both together, as key and value

blocks.caption: Word counter using .items()
blocks.explain: counts.get(w, 0) + 1 means 'whatever we had before, plus one' (starting at 0). Then .items() lets us loop over each key and value together.

blocks.question: How do you get the value stored under the key "name" in a dictionary called d?
blocks.explain: You look up a value with square brackets and the key: d["name"].

blocks.question: What does scores.get("Sam", 0) do if "Sam" is NOT in the dictionary?
blocks.explain: .get() returns the default you give (here 0) when the key is missing, instead of crashing.

blocks.question: Which helper lets you loop over both keys AND values at the same time?
blocks.explain: .items() gives you each key and its value together, perfect for 'for key, value in d.items():'.

blocks.title: Look up a favorite food
blocks.instructions: The dictionary 'favorites' maps people to their favorite food. Print Maya's favorite food using favorites["Maya"].
blocks.starter:
favorites = {"Maya": "pizza", "Leo": "tacos"}
# print Maya's favorite food below
blocks.passMsg: Nice lookup — you found Maya's favorite in a snap!
blocks.failMsg: Use favorites["Maya"] inside a print() to show the value.

blocks.title: Add a new stat
blocks.instructions: The hero dictionary needs a 'shield' stat. Add shield with the value 10, then print the whole hero dictionary.
blocks.starter:
hero = {"name": "Nova", "hp": 80}
# add a shield stat of 10, then print hero
blocks.passMsg: Shield equipped! You added a new key→value pair.
blocks.failMsg: Set hero["shield"] = 10, then print(hero) so 'shield' shows up.

challenge.title: Build a mini contact book
challenge.instructions: Make a dictionary called contacts with at least two names mapped to phone numbers. Then use a loop with .items() to print each contact like: Mom: 555-1234. Use .get() to safely look up someone who might not be there too!
challenge.starter:
contacts = {"Mom": "555-1234"}
# add another contact, then loop and print each one
challenge.hint: Loop with 'for name, number in contacts.items():' and print(name + ": " + number) inside the loop.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Your contact book works! You can now store, find, and loop through key→value data like a pro.
